text: HE et al.: AUTOMATICALLY IDENTIFYING CVE AFFECTED VERSIONS WITH PATCHES AND DEVELOPER LOGS 911

We use it against the nf _ conntrack _ core.c ﬁle to judge the existence of this vulnerability. Through the above process we can accurately justify whether there is an unpatched CVE vulnerability (i.e., without being repatched) after the maximum committed version.
Next, we present our algorithm for determining V upto with

our running example in Fig. 4 . Here, V 4 . 0 . 4 is the highest version of the committed version set. First, we determine whether the unpatched code still exists in major versions after V 4 . 0 . 4 based on exact matching. If it does not exist, it means the vulnerability has been ﬁxed in all versions after V 4 . 0 . 4 , so V 4 . 0 . 4 is regarded as V upto ( E ) . On the other hand, if unpatched code exists in a later major version, say V 5 . 0 , we will check if a repatch has been applied to it to avoid false reporting. Speciﬁcally, we ﬁrst check whether there is an unpatch status in the git log list of the vulnerable ﬁle. if it does not exist, repatch cannot exist either, so the last unpatched version will be regarded as V upto ( I ) . However, if unpatch exists, we will check if a repatch exists in the git log list indexed by the same vulnerable ﬁle, the author or the committer of the patch, and accordingly locate the diff hunk, as described in the paragraph above. If V 5 . 0 has been repatched, it does not have this vulnerability any more, so V 4 . 0 . 4 is regarded as V upto ( E ) . On the contrary, if the last unpatched version, say V 5 . 0 . 3 , has not been repatched, and it will be regarded as V upto ( I ) . Therefore, we accurately determined the boundary of the vulnerable versions on the far right of the version tree.
C. Determine the Last Vulnerable Version in Each Branch (R2)
The committed version set output from R1 of our tool does not

giveusthecompleteknowledgeonthevulnerableversions.First, some branches in the version tree may correspond to non-long- term stable versions [39] , and they are no longer maintained. Therefore, even though the versions in these branches are still vulnerable with respect to a CVE, they are no longer patched. In other words, although not covered in the committed version set, there may be vulnerable versions in these branches. Second, some versions may have no changelogs submitted, so they have slipped out from our detection, because our tool uses changelogs as the input to generate the committed version set.
To address the problem, intuitively we can check each version

against the patch. This is clearly inefﬁcient and unscalable. Instead, we leverage the following heuristics: (1) in each version branch, the release time of a minor version V i.j.k must be later than the major version V i.j or the earlier minor version V i.j.k − 1 , (2) a changelog states which vulnerabilities it has ﬁxed and it is released together with the current software version. For example, if V 4 . 14 . 177 in Fig. 5 is a committed version (because its changelog states the vulnerability has been ﬁxed), the vul- nerability must not exist in V 4 . 14 . 178 and the other later minor version. The similar examples include V 4 . 19 . 116 , V 5 . 4 . 33 etc.
Let us denote the disclosure time of the CVE as T CV E . As
reported by Li et al. [40] , CVE publication time in NVD is not the same as CVE disclosure time, but the median difference is only

one month, so that we can use CVE publication time to approx- imate its disclosure time. When the CVE is released, it may or maynothavepatchesavailable.Weuse T CV E toquicklyidentify the potentially vulnerable versions in each branch. Speciﬁcally, for each branch, we ﬁrst compare T E , which is the release time of its last version V i.j.E , with T CV E . If T E < T CV E , that is, version V i.j.E was released before the CVE, we then determine whether V i.j.E contains the vulnerable code by matching against the patch. If it is an exact match (not patched), we directly take V i.j.E as V i.j.U ( I ) of this branch. If it is a partial match, we will use the method in the next subsection to verify whether V i.j.E has the vulnerability. Otherwise, we will look for the boundary in the earlier versions backward,If the boundary does not exist, then V i.j.E is considered as V i.j.U . Otherwise we refer to the last unpatched version as V i.j.U , and verify that the patching record oftheversionfollowingtheboundaryversionismissingfromthe changelog. Therefore, we accurately determined the boundary of the vulnerable versions in each branch of the version tree.
D. Determine the First Vulnerable Version Number of CVE-ID (R3)
For a given CVE-ID, once we discover V from and V upto , the

boundary containing all the vulnerable versions is determined. However, it is very challenging to identify the ﬁrst vulnerable version V from . On the one hand, we should not immediately label the minimum committed version reported by changelog (e.g., V 3 . 0 . 6 in Fig. 4 ) as V from , because early versions might not have the corresponding changelog or the changelog was incomplete. On the other hand, open-source code versions are continuously updated and released and for some early versions, the code related to the CVE vulnerability may have gone through many changes, so that they can not be matched exactly with the patches. Recall that it is a partial match when a diff hunk does not completely match the corresponding source code. In theory,
